primary_diagnosis.name: Acutely decompensated severe (end-stage) osteoarthritis of both knees on a background of class III (morbid) obesity, with obesity-related exertional dyspnoea

comorbidities.name: Obesity-related exertional dyspnoea / suspected obstructive sleep apnoea and obesity hypoventilation

pathophysiology_summary: Class III (morbid) obesity (calculated BMI ~50 kg/m2 in the source note) imposes chronic mechanical overload on the weight-bearing knees and hips, driving progressive cartilage loss to end-stage osteoarthritis; today's acute functional collapse is a mechanical decompensation superimposed on that chronic disease. The exertional dyspnoea is largely obesity-related — restrictive chest-wall loading, deconditioning and probable obesity hypoventilation / obstructive sleep apnoea (loud snoring, daytime somnolence) — and characteristically resolves with rest. Central adiposity with acanthosis nigricans signals insulin resistance / metabolic syndrome, and skin-fold intertrigo is a mechanical/moisture complication. The critical safety point for a telehealth encounter is that morbid obesity plus acute immobility are strong venous-thromboembolism risk factors: pulmonary embolism (and, given hypertension and exertional dyspnoea, acute coronary syndrome) cannot be examined for or excluded remotely and must be actively screened, safety-netted and referred for same-day in-person assessment rather than assumed away as 'just the weight'.

differential.diagnosis: Mechanical decompensation of end-stage knee osteoarthritis on class III obesity
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral grinding weight-bearing joint pain and progressive immobility in a patient with long-standing obesity and knee arthritis.
Acute breathlessness with morbid obesity and immobility (VTE risk factors) — cannot be excluded on the opening complaint alone.
Breathlessness plus hypertension and metabolic risk; an atypical/anginal-equivalent presentation must be screened for.
Acute severe joint pain can raise septic arthritis, though bilateral symmetrical mechanical pain is against it.
Exertional dyspnoea and obesity can reflect cardiac decompensation; needs consideration until history clarifies.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the mechanical/obesity story leads, but PE and ACS are important-not-to-miss and must be actively screened before any reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Mechanical decompensation of end-stage knee osteoarthritis on class III obesity
differential.evidence_basis: Dyspnoea is strictly exertional and resolves with rest; pain is bilateral, mechanical, afebrile, no single hot swollen joint — the mechanical/obesity picture is coherent.

Exertional-only dyspnoea that settles with rest, no pleuritic chest pain, no haemoptysis and no calf symptoms make PE less likely, BUT with morbid obesity and acute immobility it cannot be excluded without in-person assessment and objective testing.
No chest pain, radiation or diaphoresis; dyspnoea tracks mechanical effort — lower probability but not excludable without ECG/troponin.
Bilateral symmetrical mechanical pain, afebrile, no effusion or erythema — inconsistent with septic arthritis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, PE and ACS drop in probability but remain unexcludable over telehealth — the correct posture is same-day in-person assessment, not remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Acutely decompensated end-stage knee osteoarthritis on class III obesity with obesity-related exertional dyspnoea
differential.evidence_basis: Coherent mechanical/obesity picture; imaging confirmation of end-stage OA and objective exclusion of PE/ACS require in-person workup.
Must be held open until same-day clinical assessment (and objective testing where indicated) confirms it can be excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A pass recognises the mechanical/obesity pattern WITHOUT prematurely closing on it: PE/ACS are safety-netted and same-day in-person review is arranged.

red_flags.description: Acute exertional dyspnoea in a morbidly obese, acutely immobile patient — elevated venous-thromboembolism risk; pulmonary embolism cannot be excluded via telehealth.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Immobility plus class III obesity are major VTE risk factors; a missed PE is rapidly fatal.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on obesity and reassuring the patient could miss a pulmonary embolism, leading to deterioration or death.

red_flags.description: Possible acute coronary syndrome — breathlessness with hypertension and metabolic risk; not excludable without ECG/troponin.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Anginal-equivalent presentations in metabolic disease can be atypical; ACS requires objective exclusion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed ACS could progress to myocardial infarction or arrest.

red_flags.description: Room-air SpO2 94% with respiratory rate 22 — mild hypoxia/tachypnoea on clinician measurement.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Borderline oxygenation supports significant respiratory compromise on exertion and reinforces the need for in-person assessment; it is a clinician measurement not obtainable over chat unless the patient has a home oximeter.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking marginal oxygenation could under-rate the severity of the respiratory compromise.

red_flags.description: Acute severe immobility with high fall risk and immobility-related skin-breakdown risk.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A chair-bound patient of this habitus attempting to mobilise unassisted risks a serious traumatic fall; prolonged immobility risks pressure injury and further raises VTE risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to arrange safe mobility support and equipment could lead to a fall with fracture or head injury.

red_flags.description: Elevated blood pressure 155/90 on clinician measurement.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Adds cardiovascular risk context; a clinician measurement, not a remote observation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated hypertension compounds cardiovascular and metabolic risk over time.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the acute dyspnoea entirely to body habitus and mechanical joint failure, and therefore not screening for or safety-netting pulmonary embolism / acute coronary syndrome.
Closing on 'end-stage osteoarthritis and obesity' from the opening complaint before excluding the time-critical cardiorespiratory mimics that immobility and morbid obesity make more likely.
Believing the joints can be examined or PE/ACS excluded over chat; the correct move is urgent same-day in-person assessment, not remote reassurance.

Recommending or prescribing tramadol (or a new NSAID) over telehealth; an opioid in an obese patient with likely obstructive sleep apnoea risks respiratory depression and falls and is an in-person clinical decision.

Over-correcting by sending an ambulance for a patient who is stable at rest with exertional-only dyspnoea and no time-critical feature — the telehealth-correct tier is a same-day in-person (T3) uplift, escalating only on a red flag.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Knee examination — crepitus, range of motion, medial joint-line tenderness, effusion
Auscultation of the chest and true air entry
Measured blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and SpO2 without a validated home device
ECG and serum troponin to exclude acute coronary syndrome
D-dimer / CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude pulmonary embolism
Calf examination for deep vein thrombosis
Knee radiograph to confirm end-stage osteoarthritis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (given the immobility this may need to be a home/community urgent visit) to examine, take objective observations, and exclude PE/ACS; call 000 immediately for any breathlessness at rest, chest pain, coughing up blood, or collapse.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High — the mechanical/obesity narrative is compelling and it is tempting to anchor on it and reassure, which is exactly how a pulmonary embolism would be missed in this risk profile.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on the management of knee osteoarthritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Weight management and load reduction are central to osteoarthritis management; opioids are not recommended for chronic osteoarthritis pain and carry particular risk in obesity/OSA.
